title: National AI Legislative Framework (White House)
summary: The Trump Administration released a comprehensive national AI legislative framework on March 20, 2026, outlining seven guiding principles for Congress to shape federal AI policy. The framework calls for broad preemption of state AI laws, child protections, intellectual property safeguards, and limits on developer liability.
key_facts: Released March 20, 2026, fulfilling a directive from Trump's December 2025 executive order on state AI laws
Calls on Congress to preempt state AI laws that 'unduly burden' AI development, while preserving state enforcement of general consumer protection laws
Explicitly states Congress should NOT create any new federal rulemaking body to regulate AI
Calls for regulatory sandboxes allowing AI companies to apply for exemptions from federal regulations
House Speaker Mike Johnson and Majority Leader Steve Scalise immediately endorsed the framework; California Gov. Newsom's office criticized it
raw_content: The four-page framework, directed by Trump's December 2025 executive order and developed by White House AI czar David Sacks and OSTP Director Michael Kratsios, lays out the administration's legislative priorities for Congress. It calls for preemption of state AI laws deemed 'undue burdens,' while carving out exceptions for state enforcement of general consumer protection and child safety laws. The framework explicitly opposes creation of any new federal AI regulatory body, instead favoring a sector-specific approach using existing agencies. It also calls for regulatory sandboxes, streamlined data center permitting, AI workforce development, and anti-censorship provisions. House Republican leadership immediately endorsed the framework, though bipartisan Senate passage remains uncertain.
tags: agency:White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP)

title: TRUMP AMERICA AI Act (Discussion Draft) — The Republic Unifying Meritocratic Performance Advancing Machine Intelligence by Eliminating Regulatory Interstate Chaos Across American Industry Act
summary: Sen. Marsha Blackburn (R-TN) released a nearly 300-page discussion draft of the TRUMP AMERICA AI Act on March 18, 2026, proposing a sweeping federal AI standard to replace the patchwork of state regulations. The bill imposes a duty of care on AI developers, sunsets Section 230, and incorporates bipartisan child safety and creator protection legislation.
key_facts: Released March 18, 2026 as a discussion draft; not yet formally introduced or referred to committee
Spans nearly 300 pages and incorporates multiple previously introduced bipartisan bills including KOSA (S.1748) and the NO FAKES Act (S.1367)
Proposes sunsetting Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, going further than prior reform proposals
Requires third-party audits of 'high-risk' AI systems to detect discrimination based on political affiliation
Includes a provision clarifying that unauthorized use of copyrighted works in AI training is NOT fair use under the Copyright Act
raw_content: The discussion draft codifies principles from Trump's December 2025 executive order and seeks to establish a single federal rulebook for AI. It places a 'duty of care' on AI developers to prevent and mitigate foreseeable harms to users, and proposes sunsetting Section 230 liability protections for online platforms. The bill incorporates the Kids Online Safety Act (S.1748) and the NO FAKES Act (S.1367), addressing child safety and digital likeness protections respectively. It also requires third-party audits of high-risk AI systems for political viewpoint discrimination, mandates quarterly corporate reporting on AI-related hiring and layoffs, and requires the Energy Department to protect ratepayers from data center electricity cost increases. The draft has not yet been formally introduced and referred to a committee.

title: S.2615 — Validation and Evaluation for Trustworthy (VET) Artificial Intelligence Act
summary: The VET AI Act directs the NIST Director to develop voluntary guidelines and specifications for internal and external assurances of AI systems, furthering the goals of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework. The bill was introduced by Senators Hickenlooper and Capito and referred to the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.
key_facts: Directs NIST Director to develop voluntary technical guidelines for AI system testing, evaluation, validation, and verification (TEVV)
Defines 'external AI assurance' as independent third-party evaluation to verify functionality and identify vulnerabilities or negative societal impacts
Explicitly builds on the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and the AI Safety Institute
Introduced by Sen. Hickenlooper (D-CO) and Sen. Capito (R-WV), demonstrating bipartisan support
Referred to the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation
raw_content: The bill tasks NIST with developing consensus-driven, evidence-based voluntary technical guidelines for testing, evaluation, validation, and verification (TEVV) of AI systems, calibrated to the intended application, use-case, and risk profile of each system. It distinguishes between 'internal' AI assurance (self-evaluation by the deploying party) and 'external' AI assurance (independent third-party evaluation), and defines key terms including 'AI system,' 'deployer,' and 'developer.' The legislation explicitly builds on and furthers the goals of the existing NIST AI Risk Management Framework. By establishing voluntary assurance standards, the bill aims to increase trust and adoption of AI systems while providing accountability and governance mechanisms without imposing mandatory compliance burdens.
tags: agency:U.S. Senate / National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)

title: S.3410 — A bill to establish Federal agency technology and artificial intelligence talent teams to improve competitive service hiring practices
summary: S.3410, introduced by Sen. Kim and referred to the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs, would authorize federal agencies to establish dedicated technology and AI talent teams to improve competitive service hiring for AI and technology positions. The bill also empowers OPM to create a government-wide Federal Technology and AI Talent Team to coordinate pooled hiring across agencies.
key_facts: Authorizes federal agencies to establish Technology and AI Talent Teams at agency or component level
Empowers OPM to create a government-wide Federal Technology and AI Talent Team for pooled hiring
Allows subject matter experts to develop and administer position-specific assessments for AI roles in competitive service
Introduced by Sen. Kim and referred to the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs
Talent teams may include positions supporting AI governance, innovation, and risk management
raw_content: The bill authorizes individual federal agencies to establish one or more 'Technology and AI Talent Teams' at the agency or component level, staffed with recruiters, assessment experts, subject matter experts, and non-technical personnel supporting AI governance and risk management. These teams would facilitate competitive service hiring for technology and AI roles, including through pooled hiring actions and shared certificates of eligibles. The Office of Personnel Management (OPM) would be empowered to establish a government-wide Federal Technology and AI Talent Team to support agency-level teams, lead cross-government AI hiring efforts, and scale best practices. Subject matter experts would be authorized to develop and administer position-specific technical assessments for AI roles. The bill addresses a recognized gap in federal AI workforce capacity at a time of rapid AI adoption across government.
tags: agency:U.S. Senate / Office of Personnel Management (OPM)

title: S.1638 — Protection Against Foreign Adversarial Artificial Intelligence Act of 2025
summary: S.1638 prohibits the use of DeepSeek to carry out federal contracts and requires the Secretary of Commerce, in consultation with the Secretary of Defense, to submit a detailed report on national security threats posed by AI platforms affiliated with countries of concern. The bill targets foreign adversarial AI platforms including large language models and generative AI.
key_facts: Explicitly prohibits use of DeepSeek AI platform to carry out any federal contract
Requires Commerce Secretary (with DoD consultation) to report on national security threats from foreign AI platforms within one year of enactment
Covers large language models and generative AI platforms based in or affiliated with 'countries of concern' (China, Russia, Iran, North Korea)
Report must include analysis of foreign government censorship laws that could influence AI applications
Referred to Senate Armed Services and Commerce committees
raw_content: The bill contains two primary operative sections: a prohibition on the use of DeepSeek (the Chinese AI platform) in the performance of any federal contract, and a mandate for a comprehensive national security threat assessment of foreign AI platforms. The required Commerce Department report must analyze censorship laws and capacities of foreign governments that could access or influence AI applications, and must be submitted within one year of enactment to the Senate and House Armed Services, Commerce, and Energy and Commerce Committees. The bill defines 'country of concern' by reference to the existing 'covered nation' definition in Title 10 of the U.S. Code, which includes China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. The legislation reflects growing congressional concern about data security and influence risks from AI tools developed in adversarial nations.
tags: agency:U.S. Senate / Department of Commerce / Department of Defense

title: S.3952 — A bill to establish artificial intelligence standards, metrics, and evaluation tools, to support AI research, development, and capacity building, and to promote innovation
summary: S.3952, introduced in the 119th Congress, would establish federal AI standards, metrics, and evaluation tools while supporting AI research, development, and capacity-building activities. The bill is designed to promote innovation by ensuring companies of all sizes can compete in the AI industry.
key_facts: Would establish federal AI standards, metrics, and evaluation tools for the 119th Congress (2025-2026)
Includes provisions to support AI research, development, and capacity-building activities
Explicitly designed to promote innovation by ensuring companies of all sizes can succeed in the AI industry
Part of a broader wave of 119th Congress AI legislation focused on standards-based rather than prescriptive regulatory approaches
Introduced in the U.S. Senate as part of the 119th Congress second session
raw_content: The bill takes a pro-innovation approach to federal AI governance by focusing on the development of standards, metrics, and evaluation tools rather than imposing prescriptive regulations. It supports AI research and development activities and includes capacity-building provisions intended to level the playing field for smaller companies competing against large incumbents. The legislation reflects a broader congressional effort to ensure the U.S. maintains its competitive edge in AI by investing in the technical infrastructure—standards and evaluation frameworks—needed to support responsible and scalable AI deployment. The bill aligns with the administration's preference for sector-specific, standards-based governance over new regulatory bodies.

title: Council of the EU Agrees Negotiating Position on Digital Omnibus AI Regulation (Omnibus VII)
summary: On 13 March 2026, the Council of the EU adopted its negotiating mandate on the Digital Omnibus AI proposal, authorising the Council presidency to begin trilogue negotiations with the European Parliament and Commission. The mandate supports adjusting the timeline for high-risk AI system obligations by up to 16 months, conditional on the availability of harmonised standards and compliance tools.
key_facts: Council adopted its negotiating mandate on 13 March 2026, part of the 'Omnibus VII' legislative package.
Proposes adjusting the high-risk AI compliance timeline by up to 16 months, conditional on availability of harmonised standards.
Extends SME exemptions to small mid-cap companies (up to 750 employees / €150M turnover).
Adds a new Commission obligation to provide guidance minimising compliance burden for high-risk AI operators under sectoral harmonisation legislation.
Trilogue negotiations with the European Parliament are now set to begin; the AI Act itself remains fully operative in the interim.
raw_content: The Council's general approach endorses the Commission's November 2025 Digital Omnibus proposal to delink the August 2026 high-risk AI enforcement date from a fixed calendar and instead tie it to the Commission's confirmation that harmonised standards and support tools are available. The mandate also extends SME regulatory exemptions to small mid-cap companies (up to 750 employees or €150M turnover), expands the ability to process sensitive personal data for bias detection, and reinforces the AI Office's supervisory powers. The Council added a new obligation requiring the Commission to provide guidance to operators of high-risk AI systems covered by sectoral harmonisation legislation. Trilogue negotiations with the European Parliament are now set to begin, with no final text yet agreed. The backstop compliance deadlines under the proposal are 2 December 2027 (Annex III high-risk systems) and 2 August 2028 (Annex I product-embedded systems).

title: European Commission Publishes Second Draft Code of Practice on Marking and Labelling of AI-Generated Content
summary: On 5 March 2026, the European Commission published the second draft of the voluntary Code of Practice on Marking and Labelling of AI-Generated Content, advancing implementation of Article 50 of the EU AI Act. The Code is expected to be finalised by June 2026, ahead of Article 50 transparency obligations becoming enforceable on 2 August 2026.
key_facts: Second draft published 5 March 2026; first draft was published 17 December 2025.
Covers machine-readable marking of AI-generated content by providers and deepfake/AI-text labelling obligations for professional deployers.
Final Code of Practice expected by June 2026; Article 50 obligations become enforceable 2 August 2026.
The Code is voluntary but designed to guide compliance with binding Article 50 AI Act transparency requirements.
Complementary Commission guidelines on transparent AI systems (scope, definitions, exceptions) are also in preparation for Q2 2026 publication.
raw_content: The Code of Practice is a voluntary instrument developed by independent experts under the facilitation of the AI Office to help providers and deployers of generative AI systems comply with the transparency obligations in Article 50 of the EU AI Act. The draft covers two main areas: rules for providers of generative AI systems on marking AI-generated or manipulated content in machine-readable formats to enable detection, and obligations for professional deployers to label deepfakes and AI-generated text on matters of public interest. The AI Office collected stakeholder feedback on the first draft (published December 2025) through a series of workshops before issuing this second draft. The final Code is expected by June 2026, with the underlying Article 50 obligations becoming enforceable on 2 August 2026. Complementary guidelines on transparent AI systems clarifying scope, definitions, and exceptions are also under preparation and will be published in Q2 2026.

title: European Commission Study on Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare: Digital Health Technologies in Europe
summary: On 17 March 2026, the European Commission published a major study on AI deployment in healthcare across all EU Member States, identifying key challenges and accelerators for clinical adoption. The study informs Commission policy on AI in healthcare under the AI Act and the Apply AI Strategy.
key_facts: Published 17 March 2026 by DG Health and Food Safety, with Open Evidence and PwC as co-authors.
Identifies four challenge categories: technological/data, legal/regulatory, organisational/business, and social/cultural.
Finds that despite AI tool availability, deployment in clinical practice remains slow across EU Member States.
Designed to support Commission action under the AI Continent Action Plan and Apply AI Strategy.
Healthcare is recognised as a strategic sector in both the EU Competitiveness Compass and the AI Continent Action Plan.
raw_content: Produced by the Directorate-General for Health and Food Safety in collaboration with Open Evidence and PwC, the study uses a mixed-methods approach—literature review and stakeholder consultation—to map the development, adoption, and use of AI technologies across EU healthcare systems. It identifies four categories of challenges to clinical AI deployment: technological and data-related issues, legal and regulatory complexities (including AI Act compliance), organisational and business obstacles, and social and cultural barriers. The study also highlights successful accelerator strategies employed by hospitals globally to overcome these obstacles. Its findings are intended to support the Commission in identifying policy gaps and taking action to achieve effective AI integration in healthcare, aligned with the AI Continent Action Plan and the Apply AI Strategy. Healthcare is designated a strategic sector in the EU's Competitiveness Compass.
tags: agency:European Commission – Directorate-General for Health and Food Safety

title: European Commission AI Office: 2026 Guidelines Roadmap for AI Act Implementation
summary: The European Commission's AI Office published a roadmap of guidelines to be developed throughout 2026 to support AI Act compliance, covering high-risk classification, transparency requirements, serious incident reporting, and the AI Act's interplay with other EU legislation including GDPR.
key_facts: AI Office to publish guidelines on high-risk classification, Article 50 transparency, and serious incident reporting in 2026.
Joint guidelines with the European Data Protection Board on AI Act/GDPR interplay are planned.
Commission will prioritise guidance on research exemptions under Articles 2(6) and 2(8) for pre-clinical and medical device contexts.
Guidelines are being developed in parallel with the Digital Omnibus legislative process.
Guidance aims to reduce compliance uncertainty ahead of the August 2026 full application date.
raw_content: In the context of the AI Act's implementation and the proposed Digital Omnibus, the AI Office announced a comprehensive set of forthcoming guidelines designed to provide clear and practical compliance instructions for businesses and national authorities. The planned 2026 guidelines include: practical application of the high-risk classification under Article 6 and Annex III; practical application of transparency requirements under Article 50; guidance on serious incident reporting for providers of high-risk AI systems; practical application of high-risk requirements (risk management, data governance, human oversight, etc.); obligations for providers and deployers of high-risk AI systems; and joint guidelines with the European Data Protection Board on the interplay of the AI Act and EU data protection law. The Commission also committed to prioritising guidance on the AI Act's research exemptions under Articles 2(6) and 2(8), particularly for pre-clinical research and medical device development. These measures are intended to ensure smooth, predictable implementation and support innovation.

title: Digital Europe Programme Amended to Continue Deployment of Innovative Digital Capacities Across the EU
summary: On 19 March 2026, the European Commission announced an amendment to the Digital Europe Programme to adapt it to Europe's evolving digital needs, including continued support for AI infrastructure and capacity deployment across Member States.
key_facts: Digital Europe Programme amendment announced 19 March 2026.
Adapts programme priorities to align with the AI Continent Action Plan and Apply AI Strategy.
Supports AI Factories and Gigafactories providing supercomputing access to SMEs and startups.
Complements AI Act implementation by building the enabling infrastructure and support ecosystem.
Part of the EU's broader strategy to combine regulatory frameworks with strategic investment in AI.
raw_content: The Digital Europe Programme amendment, announced on 19 March 2026, updates the programme's scope and priorities to reflect the EU's current digital strategy, including the AI Continent Action Plan and the Apply AI Strategy. The amendment is intended to sustain investment in AI infrastructure, digital innovation hubs, and capacity-building initiatives across Member States. This follows the Commission's broader strategy of positioning Europe as a global AI leader by combining regulatory frameworks (the AI Act) with strategic investment instruments. The programme supports AI Factories, AI Gigafactories, and European Digital Innovation Hubs that provide SMEs and public sector entities with access to AI computing resources and expertise. The amendment complements the ongoing AI Act implementation by ensuring the enabling infrastructure and support ecosystem are in place.

title: EU AI Act Full Application Approaching: High-Risk System Obligations and August 2026 Enforcement Framework
summary: With the EU AI Act's full application date of 2 August 2026 approaching, the European Commission has confirmed the scope of obligations activating on that date, including comprehensive requirements for high-risk AI systems under Annex III, Article 50 transparency obligations, and the complete market surveillance framework—subject to potential modification by the pending Digital Omnibus.
key_facts: 2 August 2026 is the primary enforcement date for most remaining AI Act obligations, including Annex III high-risk system requirements.
Article 50 transparency obligations (AI disclosure, synthetic content labelling, deepfake identification) become enforceable on 2 August 2026.
Member States must have at least one national AI regulatory sandbox operational by 2 August 2026 (Article 57).
CEN/CENELEC harmonised standards are now targeted for Q4 2026, after missing the August 2025 deadline, creating compliance uncertainty.
The pending Digital Omnibus could conditionally delay high-risk obligations by up to 16 months, with backstop dates of 2 December 2027 and 2 August 2028.
raw_content: The AI Act entered into force on 1 August 2024 and will be fully applicable on 2 August 2026, with the August 2026 date triggering the broadest wave of obligations under the regulation. These include full compliance requirements for Annex III high-risk AI systems (covering biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, law enforcement, migration, and justice), deployer obligations, conformity assessment procedures, post-market monitoring, incident reporting, and the complete market surveillance framework. Article 50 transparency obligations—requiring disclosure of AI interactions, labelling of synthetic content, and deepfake identification—also become enforceable on this date. The Digital Omnibus, if adopted before August 2026, could conditionally delay high-risk obligations by up to 16 months. Member States are also required to have at least one national AI regulatory sandbox operational by 2 August 2026 under Article 57. CEN/CENELEC harmonised standards, critical for legal certainty, are now targeted for Q4 2026, creating residual compliance uncertainty.

title: NIST AI 800-4: Challenges to the Monitoring of Deployed AI Systems
summary: NIST published NIST AI 800-4, a new standard addressing the growing need to monitor AI systems after deployment in commercial and government applications. The publication identifies that best practices, validated methodologies, and common terminology for post-deployment AI monitoring remain nascent.
key_facts: Published March 6, 2026 under the NIST Trustworthy and Responsible AI series as NIST AI 800-4.
Identifies post-deployment monitoring as crucial for validating reliable operation, tracking unforeseen outputs, and visibility into unexpected consequences.
Highlights 'lack of information sharing' as a persisting issue, citing the 2024 Foundational Model Transparency Index.
Recommends ongoing monitoring as a 'public process that tries to anticipate future misuse rather than reacting as new implicit capabilities are discovered.'
Covers AI systems integrated into both commercial and government applications, with keywords: post-deployment monitoring, system monitoring, generative AI.
raw_content: NIST AI 800-4 addresses the critical gap between pre-deployment AI evaluations — conducted in controlled environments — and the real-world need for continuous post-deployment monitoring. The document identifies three core functions of post-deployment monitoring: validating reliable operation, tracking unforeseen outputs from model non-determinism or dynamic inputs, and providing visibility into unexpected deployment consequences. It highlights a persistent industry challenge around lack of standardized guidance on AI incident sharing. The publication is part of NIST's Trustworthy and Responsible AI series and was authored by researchers from NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI). Its release signals a regulatory push toward lifecycle-based AI governance rather than one-time pre-release evaluations.
tags: agency:National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) — Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI)

title: GSA–NIST CAISI Partnership to Craft Evaluation Standards for AI Tools in Federal Operations
summary: The General Services Administration (GSA) and NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) announced a formal partnership to develop standardized evaluation benchmarks for AI tools used in federal government workflows. The initiative is designed to accelerate confident AI adoption across federal agencies.
key_facts: Announced March 18, 2026, formalizing a joint GSA–NIST CAISI partnership on AI evaluation standards.
Focus areas include developing common benchmarks for evaluating AI models and conducting hands-on research with federal workers.
Partnership is based in NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI).
Builds on the Trump Administration's July 2025 AI Action Plan, which featured CAISI prominently.
GSA's OneGov marketplace strategy, launched April 2025, provides agencies access to leading AI models at competitive prices.
raw_content: The GSA–NIST CAISI partnership formalizes a joint effort to develop common benchmarks for evaluating AI models and conduct hands-on research with federal workers. Anchored in NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation, the collaboration aims to streamline the process for developing AI standards applicable to government workflows. The partnership builds on the Trump Administration's July 2025 AI Action Plan, which prominently featured CAISI in crafting new AI policy. GSA's government-wide reach is combined with NIST's AI evaluation expertise to help agencies adopt AI with confidence. The initiative reflects a broader federal strategy to standardize AI procurement and deployment evaluation across agencies.
tags: agency:General Services Administration (GSA) and National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) — CAISI

title: NIST AI Agent Standards Initiative — RFI on AI Agent Security (Comment Period Closed March 9, 2026)
summary: NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) launched the AI Agent Standards Initiative on February 17, 2026, with a public comment period on AI Agent Security that closed March 9, 2026. The initiative aims to foster industry-led standards for autonomous AI agents to ensure secure, interoperable, and trustworthy adoption.
key_facts: Launched February 17, 2026 by NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI).
Public comment period on AI Agent Security RFI closed March 9, 2026.
ITL's AI Agent Identity and Authorization Concept Paper open for comment until April 2, 2026.
CAISI will host virtual listening sessions in April 2026 on sector-specific barriers to AI adoption in healthcare, finance, and education.
Initiative operates along three pillars: industry-led standards, U.S. leadership in international standards bodies, and reducing barriers to interoperable agent protocols.
raw_content: The AI Agent Standards Initiative is NIST's first major standards effort specifically targeting autonomous AI agents — systems capable of independently writing code, managing communications, and executing multi-step tasks. The initiative operates along three pillars: facilitating industry-led standards development, cementing U.S. leadership in international standards bodies, and reducing barriers to interoperable agent protocols. CAISI issued a Request for Information on AI Agent Security (closed March 9, 2026) and a companion concept paper on AI Agent Identity and Authorization (open until April 2, 2026). Sector-specific listening sessions on barriers to AI adoption in healthcare, finance, and education are planned for April 2026. The initiative coordinates with NSF and other interagency partners and is positioned as foundational to future regulatory and compliance frameworks for agentic AI.
tags: agency:National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) — Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI)

title: International AI Safety Report 2026
summary: The International AI Safety Report 2026, led by Turing Award winner Yoshua Bengio and authored by over 100 international experts, was released on February 3, 2026 as the second comprehensive global assessment of AI capabilities, risks, and safety measures. The 200-page report is backed by more than 30 countries and organisations including the EU, OECD, and United Nations.
key_facts: Released February 3, 2026; led by Turing Award winner Yoshua Bengio and authored by over 100 international experts.
200-page report with 1,451 references, backed by more than 30 countries and organisations including the EU, OECD, and United Nations.
Identifies a core policy challenge: AI capabilities evolve rapidly while scientific evidence emerges far more slowly.
Complements the UK AISI's Frontier AI Trends Report, which evaluated over 30 state-of-the-art models across cyber, biology, chemistry, and software engineering.
Emphasises the need for rigorous testing before deployment and continuous monitoring rather than one-time evaluations.
raw_content: The International AI Safety Report 2026 represents the most comprehensive multilateral scientific assessment of frontier AI risks to date, drawing on contributions from over 100 experts across more than 30 countries and international organisations. The report identifies a fundamental policy tension: AI capabilities evolve rapidly while scientific evidence emerges far more slowly, meaning acting prematurely risks entrenching ineffective policies, yet waiting for conclusive evidence may leave society vulnerable. It complements the UK AI Security Institute's inaugural Frontier AI Trends Report (December 2024), which evaluated over 30 state-of-the-art models across cyber, biology, chemistry, and software engineering domains. The report is intended to directly inform global policymaking and emphasises the need for rigorous pre-deployment testing. Its multilateral backing — including the EU, OECD, and UN — gives it significant weight as a reference document for international AI governance frameworks.
tags: agency:Alan Turing Institute / Centre for Emerging Technology and Security (CETaS), with backing from 30+ countries, EU, OECD, and UN

title: OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible AI
summary: On February 19, 2026, the OECD published its first internationally agreed, government-backed Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible AI — a 61-page report providing a structured, practical framework for enterprises to manage AI-related risks in line with the OECD AI Principles and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises. The guidance is backed by all OECD member countries, 17 partner governments, and the EU.
key_facts: Published February 19, 2026; 61-page report backed by all OECD member countries, 17 partner governments, and the EU.
Applies the six-step RBC due diligence framework to the full AI system value chain from design through decommissioning.
Targets interoperability with the EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001, and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework.
Addresses AI-specific adverse impact categories: privacy violations, bias, safety failures, and labour-market displacement.
Non-binding but represents the first internationally agreed, government-backed AI due diligence guidance; does not independently impose legal obligations beyond national laws.
raw_content: The OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible AI applies the established six-step Responsible Business Conduct (RBC) due diligence framework specifically to the AI system value chain — from design and development through deployment, operation, and decommissioning. It targets three groups of enterprises: those supplying inputs for AI development, those actively participating in the AI lifecycle, and those using AI in their operations. The guidance addresses AI-specific adverse impact categories including privacy violations, bias, safety failures, and labour-market displacement. It explicitly targets interoperability with the EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001, and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework. While non-binding, it represents an authoritative interpretive instrument adopted by OECD member governments and is expected to inform supervisory expectations and enforcement actions at the national level.
tags: agency:Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)

title: OECD Global Call for 'Governing with Artificial Intelligence' — Deadline Extended to 20 March 2026
summary: The OECD extended the deadline for its Global Call for 'Governing with Artificial Intelligence' to March 20, 2026, inviting governments worldwide to submit real-world AI use cases, policy initiatives, and practical tools for trustworthy AI deployment across government. Submissions will feed into a dedicated public repository on the OECD.AI Policy Observatory.
key_facts: Deadline extended to March 20, 2026 for the OECD Global Call for 'Governing with Artificial Intelligence.'
Builds on the OECD's recent global report examining 200 AI use cases across 11 government functions worldwide.
Submissions accepted from governments, international organisations, civil society, academia, and private sector where public sector is integrally involved.
Selected examples will be featured in a dedicated AI in Government global repository on the OECD.AI Policy Observatory.
Repository will serve as a trusted, up-to-date knowledge hub to support peer learning, policy development, and trustworthy AI adoption in government.
raw_content: The OECD's Global Call for Governing with AI is a structured data collection initiative designed to build a comprehensive, up-to-date evidence base of AI efforts in government. It invites submissions from national public servants, local government officials, international organisations, civil society, academia, and the private sector — provided the public sector is integrally involved. The initiative builds on the OECD's recent global report examining 200 AI use cases across 11 government functions worldwide. Selected submissions will be featured in a dedicated AI in Government global repository on the OECD.AI Policy Observatory, intended to serve as a trusted knowledge hub for peer learning and policy development. The extended March 20, 2026 deadline reflects strong international engagement and the OECD's ambition to scale trustworthy AI adoption in government globally.
tags: agency:Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)

title: Outline of the 15th Five-Year Plan for National Economic and Social Development of the People's Republic of China (2026–2030)
summary: China's NPC formally published the 141-page 15th Five-Year Plan on March 13, 2026, embedding AI as a central pillar of national economic strategy for 2026–2030. The plan mentions AI more than 50 times and includes a sweeping 'AI+ action plan' mandating deep integration of AI across industry, governance, healthcare, and education.
key_facts: AI is mentioned more than 50 times in the 141-page plan, quadrupling references from the 14th FYP.
The plan targets AI integration across 90% of the economy by 2030 and over 70% penetration of AI agents in six key sectors by 2027.
MIIT released the first national standard system covering the humanoid robot industry's entire lifecycle by March 2026, linked to the FYP's embodied intelligence provisions.
The plan proposes a World AI Cooperation Organization and a Belt and Road AI cooperation platform to support Global South countries in AI capacity building.
The plan calls for 'sandbox regulation' and trigger-based regulation as new regulatory methods for emerging AI business models.
raw_content: The 15th Five-Year Plan (FYP) elevates artificial intelligence from a sectoral policy item to the connective tissue of China's entire economic modernization strategy. It calls for 'decisive breakthroughs in key core technologies,' deploys AI agents with minimal human oversight, and builds hyper-scale computing clusters. The plan explicitly mandates strengthening AI governance and refining relevant laws, regulations, and ethical codes. It also introduces a proposed World AI Cooperation Organization and a Belt and Road AI cooperation platform to extend China's AI governance influence globally. Computing power receives its own dedicated chapter, and the digital economy value-added target is set at 12.5% of GDP by 2030.

title: China 2026 Policy Mix Announcement: 'AI Plus' Initiative and 10 Trillion Yuan AI Industry Target
summary: Senior Chinese officials at the NPC Two Sessions press conference on March 6, 2026 elaborated on the government's 2026 policy mix, forecasting AI-related industries will be valued at more than 10 trillion yuan by the end of the 15th Five-Year Plan period (2026–2030). The government confirmed it will continue advancing the 'AI Plus' initiative as a core economic growth engine.
key_facts: AI-related industries are forecast to exceed 10 trillion yuan in value by end of the 15th Five-Year Plan period (2026–2030).
Six emerging pillar industries—including integrated circuits, low-altitude economy, and intelligent robots—are targeted for accelerated growth.
Total investment in infrastructure including computing power is expected to exceed 7 trillion yuan (~$1 trillion USD) in 2026.
The government confirmed continuation of the 'AI Plus' initiative as a central policy instrument for economic transformation.
China set a GDP growth target of 4.5–5% for 2026, with AI-driven productivity gains cited as a key mechanism to achieve it.
raw_content: National Development and Reform Commission head Zheng Shanjie announced that AI is expected to play a pivotal role as the technology spreads rapidly across Chinese industries. The government will advance the 'AI Plus' initiative and boost six emerging pillar industries including integrated circuits, the low-altitude economy, and intelligent robots. Total infrastructure investment including computing power is expected to exceed 7 trillion yuan in 2026. The announcement signals that AI industrial policy and regulatory frameworks will be tightly coupled with fiscal and monetary policy in 2026.
tags: agency:National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) / State Council

title: MIIT National Standard System for Humanoid Robot and Embodied Intelligence Industry (First Release)
summary: By March 2026, MIIT released the first national standard system covering the humanoid robot and embodied intelligence industry's entire lifecycle, following the establishment of a dedicated Humanoid Robot and Embodied Intelligence Standardization Technical Committee in December 2025. This standard system governs AI-powered robotics from design through deployment and data governance.
key_facts: MIIT established a dedicated Humanoid Robot and Embodied Intelligence Standardization Technical Committee in December 2025.
The first national standard system covering the humanoid robot industry's entire lifecycle was released by March 2026.
China is leading formulation of IEC global standards for elder-care robots and actively shaping international norms for robot safety and data governance.
The 15th FYP designates robotics as one of eight 'strategic emerging industries,' embedding it directly in the top-level plan document for the first time.
Inclusion in the main FYP document triggers mandatory coordination across all central ministries, provincial governments, and state financial institutions.
raw_content: The MIIT's Humanoid Robot and Embodied Intelligence Standardization Technical Committee, established in December 2025, released China's first comprehensive national standard system for the humanoid robot industry by March 2026. The standards cover the entire product lifecycle and include provisions on robot safety, interoperability, and data governance. China is simultaneously leading formulation of IEC global standards for elder-care robots, following its successful standards campaigns in 5G and high-speed rail. The 15th FYP designates robotics as one of eight 'strategic emerging industries,' triggering mandatory coordination across all central ministries and state financial institutions. This standard system is a direct regulatory instrument to operationalize the FYP's embodied intelligence targets.
tags: agency:Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)

title: Action Plan for AI Integration in Manufacturing — Eight-Department Joint Plan Targeting Secure AI Core Tech Supply by 2027
summary: A joint action plan issued by eight departments including MIIT, CAC, and NDRC sets a 2027 target for secure and reliable supply of key core AI technologies, with China's AI industrial scale remaining among the world's forefront. The plan mandates deep integration of AI with the manufacturing sector and outlines specific model deployment and open-source ecosystem targets.
key_facts: Jointly issued by eight departments including MIIT, CAC, and NDRC.
Targets secure and reliable supply of key core AI technologies by 2027, with industrial scale remaining among the world's forefront.
Plans for deep application of 3–5 general-purpose large AI models in manufacturing by 2027.
Calls for creation of 100 high-quality industrial AI applications and full-coverage industry-specific large models.
Includes plans to build a world-leading open-source AI ecosystem and enhance AI security governance capabilities.
raw_content: The eight-department action plan outlines an ambitious push to deeply integrate AI with China's manufacturing sector, foster new quality productive forces, and comprehensively empower new industrialization. By 2027, the plan targets the deep application of three to five general-purpose large AI models in manufacturing and the development of specialized, full-coverage industry-specific large models. China also plans to build a world-leading open-source AI ecosystem and enhance security governance capabilities. The plan promotes coordinated development of AI chips' hardware and software, supports innovations in model training and inference, and fosters key industry-specific large models. It represents a direct regulatory and industrial policy instrument linking AI governance to national security and supply chain resilience.
tags: agency:Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT), Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC), National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC), and five other departments

title: Cybersecurity Law Amendment (Second Amendment) — AI Governance Provisions Effective January 1, 2026
summary: The Standing Committee of the 14th NPC passed the second amendment to China's Cybersecurity Law on October 28, 2025, with effect from January 1, 2026. The amendment introduces explicit AI governance provisions, state support for AI research and algorithm development, strengthened ethical oversight, and increased penalties for cyber offenses.
key_facts: Passed by the NPC Standing Committee on October 28, 2025; effective January 1, 2026.
Introduces explicit state support for AI research, algorithm development, and data infrastructure into the Cybersecurity Law for the first time.
Strengthens ethical oversight, risk monitoring, and enforcement against cyber offenses related to AI.
Increases penalties: entities violating cybersecurity information disclosure rules may be fined between RMB 10,000 and RMB 100,000.
Aligns the Cybersecurity Law with the DSL and PIPL to create a unified digital governance architecture covering data, algorithms, and AI services.
raw_content: The amended Cybersecurity Law integrates AI explicitly into China's core cybersecurity and digital governance framework for the first time. It introduces state support for AI research, algorithm development, and data infrastructure, while strengthening ethical oversight, risk monitoring, and enforcement. The amendment aligns the Cybersecurity Law with the Data Security Law (DSL) and Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) to form a unified system governing data, algorithms, and online services. Penalties for violations have been increased, with fines ranging from RMB 10,000 to RMB 100,000 for certain offenses. The update reflects Beijing's broader push to balance AI-driven innovation with strict state oversight.
tags: agency:Standing Committee of the National People's Congress (NPC)

title: CAC Provisional Measures on the Administration of Human-like Interactive Artificial Intelligence Services (Draft for Public Comment)
summary: The CAC issued draft provisional measures in December 2025 establishing regulatory requirements for privacy protection, ethical constraints, and safety safeguards specifically for empathetic and companion AI services. The measures target human-like interactive AI products that simulate emotional relationships with users.
key_facts: Issued by the CAC in December 2025 as a draft for public comment.
Targets empathetic and companion AI services with specific requirements for privacy protection, ethical constraints, and safety safeguards.
Complements existing CAC enforcement campaigns jointly conducted with MIIT targeting LLM filing failures, misinformation, and PIPL violations.
Addresses AI products that simulate emotional or social relationships with users—a category not previously covered by specific regulation.
Part of a broader wave of AI regulations taking effect in 2026, with stronger enforcement and penalties anticipated.
raw_content: The Provisional Measures on the Administration of Human-like Interactive Artificial Intelligence Services focus on a rapidly growing category of AI products—companion, empathetic, and social AI—that pose distinct risks around emotional manipulation, privacy, and user dependency. The draft establishes requirements for privacy protection, ethical constraints, and safety safeguards in this sector. It complements the broader CAC enforcement posture, which has included multiple joint enforcement campaigns with MIIT targeting non-compliant LLM filings, misinformation, and PIPL violations. The measures signal China's intent to regulate not just AI capabilities but the nature of human-AI relationships. Stronger enforcement and penalties are expected as multiple new AI laws take effect in 2026.
